title: FileIndex::walk_dir is recursive, unbounded — symlink loops cause stack overflow or infinite walk
description:
Steps to reproduce:
1. 

Expected: 
Actual: 

**Dimension:** Functional Correctness / Performance

**Location:** /home/doll/OpenClaudia/src/tools/file_index.rs lines 51-78 (walk_dir)

**Violation:**
FileIndex::walk_dir recurses via self.walk_dir(root, &path) at line 71. There is NO symlink guard: std::fs::read_dir follows symlinks by default, so a symlink cycle (e.g. /proc/1/cwd -> /, or user-created .worktrees/loop -> ..) sends the walk into infinite recursion and stack overflow. Additional defects:
1. **No depth limit.** A pathological filesystem with 1000 nested dirs blows the stack.
2. **Skip list is hardcoded and incomplete.** Line 60 skips names starting with '.', node_modules, target, __pycache__, dist, build — but MISSES vendor (Go, PHP), .venv (Python), venv, coverage, .next, .nuxt, bazel-bin, .tox, .pytest_cache, .gradle, DerivedData, Pods. A large monorepo will index GBs of artifacts.
3. **No cancellation.** Build() blocks for however long the walk takes, with no timeout or interrupt.
4. **Stored as rel + lower_rel strings** (line 74-75). A codebase with 1M files uses 2x memory; better to store lower once and compute relative-to-root on demand.
5. **Uses read_dir().flatten()** (line 55) which silently swallows I/O errors — permission-denied directories vanish without a warning.

**Mandated Refactor:**
1. Use the ignore crate (same engine as ripgrep) which handles gitignore, symlink cycles, depth limits, parallel walking, and a well-tuned default skip list out of the box.
2. Add max_depth: Option<usize> parameter, default 50.
3. Track visited inodes via HashSet<(dev, ino)> (Unix) to break cycles.
4. Emit I/O errors through tracing::warn rather than dropping them.
5. Build in a background thread; expose a progress counter so the TUI can show 'Indexed 2458 files...'.
